---
title: "Parsnip keras3 vs RLN: Approach Comparison"
format: html
---

## Architecture

| | parsnip `keras3.R` | RLN (`rln_callback.R`) |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | One hidden layer only | Architecture-agnostic — `RLNCallback` is a callback that takes any single layer; the tutorial happens to use a geometric series of layers |
| Regularization coverage | Applied globally across all weights | Applied to **one named layer** at a time; the tutorial targets only the first layer for efficiency |
| Regularization type | Static L2 weight decay **or** dropout (your choice, not both) | Per-weight adaptive strength — each weight learns its own regularization coefficient (lambda); the norm type, L1 or L2, is a fixed hyperparameter |
| How regularization is set | Fixed scalar `penalty` baked into `regularizer_l2()` at build time | A `lambdas` matrix (one value per weight in that layer) is updated every batch via gradient descent in log-scale |

## Design Pattern

- **parsnip's version** is a self-contained training function (`keras3_mlp(x, y, ...)`). It builds, compiles, and fits the model in one call, returning a fitted model. It is a tidymodels engine — stateless from the caller's perspective.
- **The RLN version** is a Keras callback (`RLNCallback`). It attaches to any model and modifies weight updates during training. The model itself is built separately; RLN only intercepts `on_batch_end`.

## Optimizer

- parsnip: Adam (default), overridable via `...`
- RLN tutorial: RMSprop — the paper found this worked better with the RLN update, since RMSprop's adaptive learning rates interact differently with the manual weight edits than Adam does

## RLN Per-Batch Update

The key idea: after the optimizer moves the weights, RLN applies a second per-weight nudge whose strength is itself learned.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A([Optimizer updates W]) --> B[Read W from kernel]
    B --> C["ΔW = W − W_prev"]
    C --> D{First batch?}

    D -- No --> E["Update λ\nλ ← λ − lr · ΔW ⊙ R_prev"]
    E --> F["Project\nλ += Θ − mean(λ)\nkeeps mean pinned to Θ"]
    F --> G

    D -- Yes --> G["Clip λ\nλ = min(λ, log|W / ∇norm|)\nprevents weight sign flip"]

    G --> H["Regularization term\nR = ∇norm(W) ⊙ exp(λ)"]
    H --> I["Apply\nW ← W − R"]
    I --> J([Write W back to kernel])
    J --> K([Store R as R_prev])
```

**Key symbols**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `W` | Weight matrix of the target layer (transposed to outputs × inputs) |
| `λ` | Lambda matrix — one learned log-scale coefficient per weight |
| `Θ` | `avg_reg` — the target mean of all lambdas (e.g. −10) |
| `lr` | `learning_rate` — step size for lambda updates (e.g. 1e6) |
| `∇norm(W)` | `sign(W)` for L1, `2W` for L2 |
| `⊙` | Element-wise multiplication |

The clipping step is the numerical safety valve: it ensures `exp(λ)` never exceeds `|W|`, so the regularization term cannot overshoot and flip a weight's sign.

## Scope

- parsnip's version is deliberately simple — single hidden layer, binary/multiclass/regression handled, designed to slot into tidymodels workflows with `tune()`-able hyperparameters.
- RLN is a research technique meant to replace (or augment) any regularization scheme on any architecture; it is not tied to a fixed structure.
